subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF Art IV, World Bank, Mo Ibrahim, V-Dem, RSF, FATF) + international press.

Africa's governance and income leader - a clean Nov 2024 turnover amid a wiretapping scandal, and the 2024 UK-Mauritius Chagos sovereignty win - against high public debt, an aging population, and a real press/civil-liberties wobble.

Standout features: strong institutions, a diversified services economy, and the Chagos settlement, against debt ~87-88% of GDP (above the legal ceiling), demographic aging (TFR ~1.4), cyclone exposure, and an election-eve social-media shutdown. Net positive.

economic.notes: A diversified, own-currency services economy, against high public debt now forcing austerity.

fiscal_state.notes: Public-sector debt ~87-88% of GDP (above the 80% statutory ceiling), with the 2025-26 budget pivoting to consolidation toward ~75% by 2030. (IMF Art IV 2025)

monetary_independence.notes: The own rupee on a managed float gives genuine, if small-economy-constrained, autonomy. (Bank of Mauritius)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The rupee is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Moderated from the post-2022 spike and broadly contained in 2025. (IMF)

banking_stability.notes: Sound and liquid, with a large offshore/global-business sector creating funding-volatility risk. (Coface; IMF)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: A diversified services-led economy (finance, tourism, ICT) with many tax treaties, exposed to external-demand swings. (OECD; IMF)

institutional.notes: Africa's strongest governance, but with a corroborated press-freedom deterioration.

rule_of_law.notes: The strongest governance in Africa (Mo Ibrahim #1) with independent courts; the wiretapping scandal exposed misuse but elections/courts held. (BTI 2026; Mo Ibrahim)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Strong, professional administration with high state capacity for its peer group. (World Bank; BTI)

press_freedom.notes: A notable deterioration - a polarised media, ICT-Act prosecutions, annual licensing, and a pre-election social-media shutdown. (RSF 2025; Civicus)

political_social.notes: Stable competitive democracy and cohesion, with a real civil-liberties wobble and aging.

political_stability.notes: The Nov 2024 landslide turnover and peaceful concession reaffirmed stable, competitive democracy. (Al Jazeera; ECN)

civil_liberties.notes: Broadly free, but the temporary election-eve social-media ban and ICT-Act harassment mark a real wobble. (Al Jazeera; Civicus)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Generally tolerant and diverse, with standard migrant-labour concerns but no systematic discrimination. (observed)

social_cohesion.notes: Stable multi-ethnic cohesion; communal politics managed peacefully. (BTI 2026)

demographics.notes: An aging headwind - median age ~39, TFR ~1.4, the 60+ share nearly doubled, pressuring pensions/fiscal. (OECD)

geopolitical.notes: A reliable, treaty-based partner with the Chagos dispute resolved.

alliance_reliability.notes: A reliable, predictable partner - the orderly Chagos deal with the UK/US underscores treaty-based diplomacy. (Reuters)

conflict_involvement.notes: No conflict; the Chagos sovereignty dispute was resolved diplomatically via the 2024 treaty. (press)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Off the FATF grey list since 2021, open economy, with a 2027 FATF review to sustain. (FATF; IMF)

physical_practical.notes: Strong infrastructure and healthcare, against acute SIDS cyclone exposure.

climate.notes: A SIDS highly exposed to cyclones, floods, and sea-level rise - climate losses ~0.8% of GDP/year, plus a 2024 drought. (World Bank CCDR)

healthcare.notes: Among Africa's best - high life expectancy and broad access, consistent with high-income status. (World Bank)

infrastructure.notes: Strong (ports, roads, telecom, finance) underpinning its offshore-hub role. (World Bank; BTI)

disaster_insurance.notes: High cyclone/flood exposure (~0.8%-of-GDP recurring losses) despite better-than-peer penetration. (World Bank CCDR)

summary: Africa's governance and income leader - the November 2024 election delivered a clean landslide turnover (the Alliance du Changement, ~62.6%) that ousted the incumbent amid the 'Missie Moustass' wiretapping scandal, and the 2024 UK-Mauritius Chagos agreement resolved a decades-old sovereignty dispute on favourable terms. Strong institutions, a diversified services economy, an own managed-float rupee, and removal from the FATF grey list (2021) anchor a positive skew. The watch items are real: public debt near 87-88% of GDP (above the legal ceiling) now forcing austerity, an aging population (TFR ~1.4), acute cyclone exposure, and a genuine press-freedom/civil-liberties wobble that several monitors read as democratic-quality erosion.
